title: Upgrading your heating controls: programmer, room stat, and TRVs
subtitle: What the controls do, what to upgrade, and what the numbers say
excerpt: Good heating controls can reduce gas consumption by 15-20% without any changes to the boiler or radiators. The standard upgrade path is a seven-day programmer, a single room thermostat in the main living area, and thermostatic radiator valves on every radiator except the one with the room stat.
sourceNotes: HHIC (Heating and Hotwater Industry Council) Best Practice Guides on heating system controls. Energy Saving Trust heating controls guidance. Ofgem consultation on smart heating controls. EST research on control upgrades and consumption reduction (2023).

content.text: A house with a modern condensing boiler but only an on/off switch burns far more gas than the same house fitted with a
on every radiator. Adding a programmer and room stat to a home with no controls cuts annual gas use by about 15 to 20%. Adding TRVs on top of basic controls saves a further 5 to 10%.

The programmer tells the boiler when to be available to provide heat and hot water. Without one, the boiler either runs continuously or is switched manually. A seven-day programmer allows the heating to come on at 6:30 am on weekdays (just before getting up) and at 7:30 am on weekends, then off at 8:30 pm. Savings come from not heating an empty house during the day and not overheating before sleep.

The room thermostat controls the boiler based on temperature in the main room. When the room reaches the set point (typically 18-20°C for a living area), the boiler shuts off. Raising the set point by 1°C increases gas consumption by approximately 6-8%. A single degree of unnecessary set-point creep adds 6-8% to the annual gas bill. The thermostat is typically mounted 1.5 m above floor level on an interior wall, away from draughts and heat sources. Do not mount it near an exterior door or in a room with south-facing glazing.

TRVs prevent the boiler from overheating rooms that don't need to be at full temperature. Set bedroom TRVs to 2-3 (approximately 16-18°C). Set the bathroom TRV to 3 or leave it fully open (bathrooms benefit from full heat during the brief period they are occupied). Set the living room TRV to fully open and control the living room temperature with the room thermostat; if the room stat is in the hallway, fit a wireless stat and put the TRV on the hallway radiator to prevent the hallway from holding heat back.

Smart heating systems (Hive, Nest, Tado, and others) add remote control. Some use geofencing, where the heating turns on as your phone gets near home. The extra saving from a smart thermostat over a good plain programmer and room stat is small, about 4 to 7%. The real win is being able to check or change the schedule from anywhere. Tado and Hive also offer weather compensation. The boiler shifts its flow temperature with the outside temperature, which helps a condensing boiler run more efficiently.
What an installer will do versus what you can do yourself

Replacing a programmer. A confident DIYer can swap a like-for-like programmer with the power off. The wiring must match. A different wiring scheme means knowing which terminal is call-for-heat, which is hot water, and which is live and neutral. You need a wiring diagram and a multimeter. If unsure, use a Gas Safe registered heating engineer.

Replacing TRVs: a competent DIYer can remove and fit a TRV on a drained system. On a pressurised sealed system, isolating the radiator with the lockshield valve and a TRV valve head allows head replacement without draining. Replacing the valve body (the piece screwed into the radiator) requires draining and carries a risk of fitting problems if the old valve has corroded.
